caps.reason: You cannot be placed abroad without a passport, so your readiness is held at needs prep until you have one in hand. It is the first thing to sort, and SA passport turnaround is usually a few weeks.

ranges.reason: Under 18 falls below the minimum age for most seasonal programmes; wait until you turn 18.
Within the 18 to 28 sweet spot that qualifies for J-1 Summer Work Travel and Canada IEC.
Past the J-1 cap but still inside the Canada IEC and UK seasonal age limits of 35.
Above 35 rules out most youth-mobility schemes; focus on employer-petitioned routes like H-2B.

reason.Valid — 18+ months remaining: Passport comfortably valid, meets the validity window for seasonal visas.
reason.Valid — under 18 months remaining: Passport valid but renew soon; some consulates want validity beyond your programme end date.
reason.Expired: Passport expired, renew at Home Affairs before applying to any sponsor.
reason.No passport: No passport yet, apply immediately; this is the first blocker for any season abroad.

reason.Current (within 6 months): Current SAPS clearance, meets the visa-ready bar for seasonal programmes.
reason.Older than 6 months: Police clearance older than 6 months; most sponsors want one issued within the last 6 months.
reason.Applied — waiting: SAPS clearance in progress; allow for the typical 4 to 8 week turnaround in your timeline.
reason.None: No police clearance, apply to SAPS now as turnaround is typically 4 to 8 weeks.

reason.Under R20k: Below the typical R45k to R65k needed to cover sponsor fees, flights and your first weeks abroad.
reason.R20k – R40k: Partway to the R45k to R65k all-in budget; line up the balance before committing.
reason.R40k – R70k: Covers the usual J-1 SWT spend through to your first paycheck.
reason.R70k+: Strong buffer for sponsor fees, flights and settling-in costs.
reason.Prefer not to say: Budget not declared; share it with us privately as it shapes which programmes are realistic.

reason.Currently enrolled full-time: Full-time enrolment meets the J-1 Summer Work Travel requirement, the flagship US seasonal route.
reason.Recently graduated (within 12 months): As a recent graduate you may still qualify with some sponsors and for Canada IEC, but J-1 SWT needs current enrolment, so confirm your eligibility window.
reason.Graduated more than 12 months ago: Graduating over a year ago closes J-1 SWT, but Canada IEC (18 to 35) and employer-petitioned H-2B remain open.
reason.Not a student: Not being a student closes the J-1 SWT route, but Canada IEC, H-2B and UK seasonal work do not require student status, so target those.

reason.Hospitality / restaurants: Hospitality is the core of seasonal hiring; resorts and restaurants fill these roles every cycle.
reason.Theme park / resort: Theme park and resort experience maps directly onto the highest-volume J-1 placements.
reason.Lifeguarding / camp: Camp and lifeguarding experience is prized for US summer programmes and often pays a premium.
reason.Agriculture / farming: Agricultural experience suits UK and Canada seasonal harvest roles where demand is steady.
reason.Customer service / call centre: Customer-facing experience transfers well to most front-of-house seasonal placements.
reason.Retail: Retail experience is solid groundwork for the customer-facing roles sponsors recruit for.
reason.Other: Confirm how your sector maps to seasonal placements; transferability varies by programme.
reason.None yet: No sector experience yet; any customer-facing or hands-on work will strengthen your placement.

rules.empty_reason: No work sectors recorded. Hospitality, resort and camp experience get placement priority, so add any customer-facing or hands-on work you have done.

ranges.reason: Under a year of customer-facing work; sponsors accept entry-level applicants but more hours help.
One to three years of relevant experience, a comfortable fit for most seasonal roles.
Three to six years of experience, a strong candidate for better-paid placements.
Six or more years of customer-facing experience, a standout seasonal applicant.

reason.No: First-time applicant, which is normal; sponsors place plenty of new arrivals each season.
reason.Yes — completed: A completed programme is a strong signal that sponsors and consulates trust returners.
reason.Yes — in progress: An application already underway gives you a head start on the next cycle.
reason.Yes — was refused: A prior refusal needs careful handling; understand the reason before reapplying.

reason.2026: Targeting 2026 means recruitment cycles are open now, so start applications immediately.
reason.2027: A 2027 target gives ample runway to line up documents and a sponsor.
reason.2028: A 2028 target is early; use the time to save and build customer-facing experience.
reason.Just exploring: Exploring only, so there is no urgency yet; plan around the season that suits you.

reason.Up to 4 months (typical SWT): Up to 4 months fits the J-1 Summer Work Travel window perfectly.
reason.4–12 months: A 4 to 12 month window opens UK seasonal and longer resort contracts.
reason.12–24 months (Canada IEC): A 12 to 24 month commitment suits the Canada IEC working holiday.
reason.Open: Flexible availability lets us match you to whichever season recruits first.

reason.Native / first language: English as a first language clears the bar for all customer-facing seasonal roles.
reason.Fluent: Fluent English is sufficient for the front-of-house roles sponsors recruit for.
reason.Conversational: Conversational English works for many roles but limits guest-facing placements.
reason.Basic: Basic English is a real blocker for customer-facing seasonal work; build it up first.
